# FFT {#sec-fft}

The IPPL `FFT` layer wraps scalable transforms and uses HeFFTe as the distributed backend. FFT support is optional at configure time and should be enabled with `IPPL_ENABLE_FFT=ON`.

## Transform types

| Transform tag | Field type | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| `CCTransform` | complex field | complex-to-complex, in-place. |
| `RCTransform` | real input, complex output | real-to-complex and complex-to-real. |
| `SineTransform` | real field | in-place sine transform. |
| `CosTransform` | real field | in-place cosine transform. |
| `Cos1Transform` | real field | in-place cosine type-I transform. |

`TransformDirection` is either `FORWARD` or `BACKWARD`.

## Backend selection

The FFT wrapper selects a HeFFTe backend from the field memory space and the configured HeFFTe features:

| Memory/backend condition | HeFFTe backend family |
|---|---|
| host with FFTW | `fftw`, `fftw_sin`, `fftw_cos`, `fftw_cos1` |
| host with MKL | `mkl`, `mkl_sin`, `mkl_cos` |
| CUDA space | `cufft`, `cufft_sin`, `cufft_cos`, `cufft_cos1` |
| HIP space with ROCm | `rocfft`, `rocfft_sin`, `rocfft_cos`, `rocfft_cos1` |
| fallback host/HIP | HeFFTe stock backends |

The wrapper copies owned field data without ghost layers into temporary LayoutLeft Kokkos views before calling HeFFTe. This is because HeFFTe operates on local boxes without IPPL ghost cells.

## HeFFTe parameters

FFT and FFT-based solver examples use `ParameterList` keys:

| Key | Type | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| `use_heffte_defaults` | `bool` | Use HeFFTe defaults instead of explicit IPPL options. |
| `use_pencils` | `bool` | Select pencil decomposition when explicit options are used. |
| `use_reorder` | `bool` | Enable/disable HeFFTe reorder option. |
| `use_gpu_aware` | `bool` | Request GPU-aware communication when supported. |
| `comm` | `int` | Communication algorithm: `a2a`, `a2av`, `p2p`, or `p2p_pl`. |
| `r2c_direction` | `int` | Shortened dimension for real-to-complex transforms. |

The `comm` enum maps to HeFFTe reshape algorithms:

| IPPL enum | HeFFTe algorithm |
|---|---|
| `a2a` | all-to-all |
| `a2av` | all-to-all-v |
| `p2p` | point-to-point |
| `p2p_pl` | pipelined point-to-point |

## Complex-to-complex example

`test/FFT/TestFFTCC.cpp` creates a complex field, applies forward and backward transforms, and checks the recovered field.

```cpp
using Field_t = ippl::Field<Kokkos::complex<double>, 3, Mesh_t, Centering_t>;
using FFT_t   = ippl::FFT<ippl::CCTransform, Field_t>;

ippl::ParameterList params;
params.add("use_heffte_defaults", true);

FFT_t fft(layout, params);
fft.transform(ippl::FORWARD, field);
fft.transform(ippl::BACKWARD, field);
```

## Real-to-complex example

`test/FFT/TestFFTRC.cpp` uses separate input and output layouts because the complex output is shortened in one dimension.

```cpp
ippl::ParameterList params;
params.add("use_heffte_defaults", true);
params.add("r2c_direction", 0);

using FFT_t = ippl::FFT<ippl::RCTransform, RealField>;
FFT_t fft(layoutInput, layoutOutput, params);

fft.transform(ippl::FORWARD, realField, complexField);
fft.transform(ippl::BACKWARD, realField, complexField);
```

If `r2c_direction == 0`, the output domain length in dimension 0 is `N0 / 2 + 1`. The analogous rule applies for directions 1 and 2.

## Sine and cosine examples

Sine, cosine, and cosine type-I transforms are in-place real-field transforms. `test/FFT/TestSine.cpp` demonstrates explicit parameters:

```cpp
params.add("use_heffte_defaults", false);
params.add("use_pencils", true);
params.add("use_reorder", false);
params.add("use_gpu_aware", true);
params.add("comm", ippl::p2p_pl);

using FFT_t = ippl::FFT<ippl::SineTransform, Field_t>;
FFT_t fft(layout, params);
fft.transform(ippl::FORWARD, field);
fft.transform(ippl::BACKWARD, field);
```

## User guidance

FFT-heavy applications should choose decompositions and grid sizes with the communication pattern in mind. For GPU runs, the build must align Kokkos, HeFFTe, MPI, and the device runtime.

HeFFTe currently supports the IPPL transform path for 2D and 3D fields. For real-to-complex transforms, users must create compatible input and output domains. For all transforms, IPPL fields may have ghost cells, but the FFT operates on the owned region.
